What do reviews for public swimming pools
tell us about society?

Reviews from visitors across Germany — in their own words

The research

Every summer, millions of people visit public pools across Germany. Some leave reviews. Inside those reviews — a window into how we live together.

We collected over 1.3 million reviews of public swimming pools across all 16 German states — individuals sharing their opinions of public spaces from their lived experiences. Then we asked: what happens when race, ethnicity, culture, religion or nationality enters the picture?

🏊
1.4M
reviews collected across 7K pools in all 16 federal states

Public pool reviews — not a survey, not an interview. Individuals sharing their opinions of public spaces from lived experience.

👥
542K
social experiences

Reviews describing encounters with other people — interactions with visitors, staff, or the felt sense of belonging in a shared space.

A review is social when it describes an encounter with or opinion about other people at the pool — not just the facilities.
🔍
8.3K
social experiences of interest

Reviews where race, ethnicity, culture, religion or nationality played a role — in how someone was treated, perceived, or made to feel.

What does racialised mean? A review is racialised when it links a behaviour, presence, or quality to someone's race, ethnicity, nationality, culture or religion — whether stated directly or implied. It can be positive, negative, or neutral. Example: "The staff had no problem with headscarves" — this links a quality to religious identity. That counts.

What did we find?

A decade of racialised reviews — and growing

Half of all 8,271 racialised reviews were written in 2023 or later. This is not a reaction to the 2015 refugee crisis — it is a pattern that has intensified year on year.

💭
67%
generalised experience

Most racialised reviews describe an ongoing atmosphere — not a single incident. This is ambient, everyday experience, not isolated events.

🔄
360+
explicitly welcoming reviews

The same pool can feel hostile to one visitor and welcoming to another — based on who they are. Both experiences are in the data.

🧭
East ≠ West
different patterns

Former East German states show more crowd and language themes; West shows more clothing and etiquette — reflecting different demographic contexts.

🏠
Guided story

Belonging — who feels at home in a public pool? In the explorer below, follow one theme across Germany. Click a state. Read what people wrote. Then ask: whose pool is it?

The question

A public swimming pool is one of the most democratic spaces in a city — open to everyone, paid for by everyone.

What happens when people with different backgrounds, expectations, and ideas of “normal” have to share the same space?

“On the one hand, a nostalgic yearning for the past — on the other hand, still desiring more acceptance — we haven’t understood what integration really asks of a shared public space.”

Niedersachsen2021–2023Etiquette · Explicit

“There is no requirement to undress in the saunas! Many young men, mostly with a migration background, sit with towels wrapped among the completely naked sauna guests. This should not be permitted.”

Sachsen-Anhalt2023+Belonging · Positive

“Beautiful and peaceful pool. The staff were very friendly and had no problem with headscarves. Thank you.”

Nordrhein-Westfalen2023+Language · Incident

“Because of my dark skin, I was treated like a criminal and spoken to exclusively in English, even though I live here and speak German. It felt like this place was only for white people.”

Brandenburg2021–2023Belonging · Generalised

“Many, many years ago, it was a decent spa with decent visitors… The spa is still good, but I don’t go back anymore.”

Rheinland-Pfalz2023+Clothing · Incident

“We travelled all the way from Wiesbaden. I wore a burkini, and I was the only one — I felt a bit uncomfortable.”

Mecklenburg-Vorpommern2017–2019Belonging · Negative

“The majority of the clientele is AfD-like. As a person of color, I would avoid this place.”

Niedersachsen2021–2023Etiquette · Explicit

“There is no requirement to undress in the saunas! Many young men, mostly with a migration background, sit with towels wrapped among the completely naked sauna guests. This should not be permitted.”

Rheinland-Pfalz2023+Clothing · Incident

“We travelled all the way from Wiesbaden. I wore a burkini, and I was the only one — I felt a bit uncomfortable.”

Mecklenburg-Vorpommern2017–2019Belonging · Negative

“The majority of the clientele is AfD-like. As a person of color, I would avoid this place.”

Sachsen-Anhalt2023+Belonging · Positive

“Beautiful and peaceful pool. The staff were very friendly and had no problem with headscarves. Thank you.”

Brandenburg2021–2023Belonging · Generalised

“Many, many years ago, it was a decent spa with decent visitors… The spa is still good, but I don’t go back anymore.”

Nordrhein-Westfalen2023+Language · Incident

“Because of my dark skin, I was treated like a criminal and spoken to exclusively in English, even though I live here and speak German. It felt like this place was only for white people.”

Why it matters

Eight thousand people wrote something down.
Here is what that might mean.

🔬

Social integration at ground level

Not in policy papers or surveys — but in the moment someone has to share a changing room with a stranger and decides to write about it. 8,271 people did.

📅

Not a refugee crisis story

Half of all racialised reviews were written in 2023 or later. The pattern predates 2015 and has intensified year on year. The tensions are continuous — not a reaction to one political moment.

🔎

Invisible to official data

No census question asks this. No survey captures it. These are experiences that happen every summer and disappear — unless someone writes them down.

🔧

What can be done with it

Understanding these patterns can inform how pools are managed, how rules are communicated, and how public spaces can be designed to serve everyone who funds them.

"Until Germans feel that sauna culture is being lost — while immigrants still feel their swimwear is being frowned upon — we haven't understood what integration really asks of a shared public space."
Interdisciplinary Institute for Societal Computing
Societal Observatory Using Novel Data Sources Lab

Research uses publicly available review data. Displayed reviews are anonymised. These are patterns in lived experience — no causal claims are made.

Deep dive — explore the data

Every state. Every theme. Every dimension.

🗺 Where — across Germany 💬 What — what is contested 🔍 How — tone & expression

Where in Germany?

Racialised reviews appear in every single state. Hover any state to see its full breakdown — tone, themes, and how experiences are expressed.

What is being contested? The topic — crowd (who is present), clothing (what people wear), belonging (who feels at home), language, staff, access, etiquette. Each review can touch multiple topics. What is the tone? Negative = unfavourable attribution to a group · Neutral = factual or demographic observation · Positive = favourable attribution to a group What happened? Generalised = describes an ongoing atmosphere or chronic impression, not tied to a specific event · Incident = something specific happened on a particular visit

Select a theme above,
then hover a state

Now that you see what is contested — how exactly is it being said?

Expression & Context

Two dimensions cut across every racialised review. How it is expressed: explicitly (a group is named — "too many foreigners", "only Arabs here now"), implicitly (meaning is coded — "clientele has changed", "not what it used to be"), or both. What kind of event: generalised (an ambient atmosphere, a chronic feeling — "every time I come, the same feeling") or incident-based (something specific happened on a particular visit). Together they shape what a review is really saying.

How expressed
ExplicitA group is named directly: "too many foreigners", "only Arabs here now", "no Germans left"
ImplicitMeaning is coded: "clientele has changed", "not what it used to be", "certain people ruin the atmosphere"
BothA single review contains both direct naming and coded language
What kind of event
GeneralisedAn ongoing atmosphere or chronic feeling: "every time I come, the same thing", "the pool has changed over the years"
IncidentA specific event on a particular visit: "last week a group refused to follow the rules", "I was turned away at the door"

16 states · 5 time periods · watch them move

← More implicit Explicit expression (%) More explicit →
↑ More generalised    More incident-based ↓
